data.title: Miller Industries: This Surprise Doesn't Change Things
data.description:
After the market closed on March 4, the management team at Miller Industries, Inc. (MLR), announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Although revenue and profitability for the company worsened year over year, results did come in above what analysts were hoping to see. On top of this, management provided some guidance for the 2026 fiscal year. While that data was limited, at least it did point toward a nice increase in revenue. Despite this, I maintain that industry conditions are challenged at the moment. While the current expectations that management has call for operational improvements, specifically with revenue climbing, I would like to see this play out a bit before getting bullish.

This is a real shame because I would love nothing more than to upgrade Miller Industries from a "Hold" to a "Buy." Since my first "Hold" article about the company, published back in September of last year, the stock is up 7.2%. That narrowly edges out the 5.3% rise of the S&P 500 over the same period. But even though you might think that this outperformance is evidence that market participants are optimistic, the fact of the matter is that the stock is not particularly cheap when we consider worsening revenue and profitability. If we do see a real turnaround, an upgrade of the stock would be sensible. But for now, I think that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice.

A Bumpy Trip

Depending on your perspective, you could argue that the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was both good and bad for Miller Industries. On the negative side, for instance, revenue contracted from $221.9 million to $171.2 million. That's a pretty substantial drop. But on the positive side, revenue actually ended up being $7 million above what analysts anticipated. For the year as a whole, meanwhile, revenue fell sharply from $1.26 billion to $790.3 million. This overall weakness for the company was driven by lower production levels aimed at mitigating inventory buildup in its distribution channel.

To provide more clarity, management has offered up their general thoughts about the state of the markets in which the company plays. They said that 2025 brought with it several challenges that are ongoing. Most notably, Miller Industries is still dealing with the fallout from the COVID-19 pandemic and the disruption it caused in global supply chains. But that's not all. Inflationary pressures, tied in large part to the aforementioned supply chain disruptions, have proven to be problematic. And then when you add on top of this the havoc associated with tariffs, it's no wonder that the company has experienced weakness. Unfortunately, the firm mentioned in its annual report that they continue to see significant pressure on global supply chains as economic uncertainty and geopolitical tensions remain at the forefront.

Military conflicts in Ukraine and throughout the Middle East have proven to be particularly disruptive. But even at the industry level, there have been issues. For instance, management said that over the last few years, there were supplier shortages and other logistics disruptions, some of which were caused by workforce disruptions and material shortages.

The fact that revenue is weak is an issue all on its own. However, it's also bleeding into profitability. Earnings per share for the company plummeted from $0.91 in the final quarter of 2024 to $0.29 in the final quarter of 2025. The good news is that this still ended up being $0.21 per share above what analysts were hoping for. But that doesn't change the fact that overall net profits dropped from $10.5 million to $3.4 million. Other profitability metrics for the company largely worsened. The one exception to this would be operating cash flow. It skyrocketed from negative $11.7 million to positive $48.8 million. But if we strip out changes in working capital, we get a plunge from $13.9 million to $6.7 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company worsened from $17.2 million to $8.4 million.

The second chart in this article shows financial performance for the 2025 fiscal year compared to 2024. I already detailed how revenue dropped. But as was the case in the final quarter on its own, net profits for the company fell hard. The same can be said of most cash flow metrics, with declines across all but operating cash flow. Hopefully, by the end of this year at least, things will become sufficiently positive enough that we can put these troubles behind us. In the introduction to this article, I mentioned that management is forecasting a rise in revenue this year. That is true. They currently believe that sales will be between $850 million and $900 million for 2026.

According to the company, in 2025, distributor inventories returned to historical average levels. Thanks to this, in 2026, the company expects that production volumes will eventually increase, eventually hitting retail activity levels in the second half of this year. Around that time, we should expect quarterly revenue for the company of about $250 million. Management also believes that this will allow gross margins to return to historical levels in the mid-13% range for 2026 in its entirety. But the problem here is that the gross profit margin for the company in 2025 was 15.2%. So we actually could see a bit of overall margin compression absent a change in the corporate structure elsewhere.

One thing that I am pleased by is the fact that, even though times are tough right now, management is actively making investments in the company's future. The firm recently completed its purchase of Omars, a producer of light-duty, medium-duty, and heavy-duty recovery vehicles and car carriers that is based in Italy. It is also seeing some success these days when it comes to its military production operations, ending 2025 with $150 million in military commitments associated with heavy-duty recovery products. However, that won't start to play a role in the overall financial picture of the business until early 2027, when production starts. And most of that revenue won't come about until 2028 and 2029.

Structurally, the company is making some changes as well. For instance, it invested €8 million into expanding production at Jige. This is a European facility that will essentially double the company's heavy-duty integration capacity there. It's also investing in improving production efficiencies at Boniface when it comes to light-duty and heavy-duty units. Then we also have the construction of a new manufacturing facility located at the company's headquarters. This is a rather substantial endeavor, with management adding more than 200,000 square feet of space to increase production capacity and achieve operational efficiencies. This will not come cheap, though. Management currently expects overall expenses here to be around $100 million. But the good news is that the company expects to cover these costs using cash flows that it generates during the construction and ramp-up process.

If we do value the company based on what management achieved in 2025 and 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. If we assumed that financial performance would go back to what was seen in 2024, the stock would look outright cheap. At some point, the business probably will move in that direction. But when it comes to the 2025 approach, the stock looks much closer to being fairly valued. It is worth noting that, in that chart, I stacked Miller Industries up against five other similar companies. And while it was the cheapest of the group when it came to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach, three of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is on an EV-to-EBITDA basis.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided right now, it is my belief that Miller Industries is an interesting prospect. It is looking increasingly likely that 2026 will mark a turnaround for the business. But until we see some real progress on that front, I believe that taking a more cautious approach to the company is warranted, especially at a time when I believe that economic conditions are pointing toward a recession. Given this, I believe that it would be most prudent to maintain the business as a "Hold" candidate.

data.title: EPOL: Invest In The Economic Success Story Of The 21st Century
data.description:
Consider for a minute, South Korea.

The country’s current economic prosperity is nothing short of a miracle. Korea spent most of the first half of the 20th century under Japanese occupation. After World War II it was split in half; a communist north, and a capitalist south.

Then the north invaded the south, kicking off a war that would last for years and see the now capital of Seoul leveled as it changed hands several times between the American and Chinese forces fighting in the peninsula.

Despite that adversity, South Korea, half of what once had been a united country, transformed into an economic powerhouse over the next 70-odd years. Companies like Samsung (SSNLF), Hyundai, SK Hynix, and LG Electronics (066570.KS) are household names worldwide. By every measure, South Korea was one of the 20th century’s greatest economic success stories.

I think Poland will be the South Korea of the 21st century. And the only easy way for Americans to play Europe’s most dynamic economy is the iShares MSCI Poland ETF (EPOL). But first a bit of history, because I’m willing to bet Poland seems a pretty random choice.

The Opportunity: Poland is Not Yet Lost

The Polish national anthem begins with the line “Poland is not yet lost.”

It speaks to the incredibly rough 219 years Poland experienced between 1772 and 1991. In the 18th century, Poland was sandwiched between Prussia (the precursor to today’s Germany), Russia, and the Hapsburg Empire of Austria.

Starting in 1772, Poland’s neighbors waged three wars of partition which saw Poland’s territory totally absorbed by the three of them by 1795. And, except for a brief stint of independence during the Napoleonic Wars, Poland disappeared from the map until 1918.

The new Polish state’s territory was where a lot of the fighting on the Eastern Front of World War I had taken place. And while the country was still getting its footing, it was invaded on both sides by Germany and the Soviet Union in 1939. After World War II, Poland became a satellite state of the Soviet Union. There was a Poland on the map once more, but the Soviets controlled it.

It remained that way until the Soviet Union fell in 1991, freeing the nations of the Eastern Bloc and leaving Poland with full independence for the first time since 1939 and Poland has not looked back since.

The country has consistently ranked as Europe’s fastest growing economy and as of January 2026 it’s set to enter the G20 by its own right as opposed to just being represented by the European Union, joining South Korea and the other 18 countries that make up 85% of the world’s GDP.

How incredible is Poland’s economic growth?

Well, take a look at this chart from the World Bank.

Since its first full year of independence in 1992, Poland has grown its economy faster than Germany, the continent’s largest economy, every single year except for 2001 and 2010.

And Poland has had only a single year of negative growth in 2020 which really had nothing to do with Poland’s economy and much more to do with COVID-19. Even then, Poland only saw a contraction of 2% in its GDP to Germany’s 41.%.

To further drive the point of Polish economic resilience home, take a look at this map from the International Monetary Fund. Poland was one of only three countries to grow its economy in 2009, during the Global Economic Crisis.

And Polish companies are beginning to break out not just into the European market but the global market. The one most Americans would likely be familiar with is CD Projekt S.A. (CDR.WA), the video game developer behind The Witcher series of games and Cyberpunk 2077.

And that brings me, at last, to the iShares MSCI Poland ETF which has seen Poland’s economic success translate into incredible returns.

The Money: A Bet on White and Red

EPOL is the third best performing international ETF over the past three years (January 31, 2023 – January 31, 2026). Its 3-year total returns (monthly) came to 157.36%, behind only Greece at 169% and Peru at 214%.

But I like a bet on the Polish economy and on EPOL because of their consistency.

Since EPOL’s inception in May 2010, it has averaged an annual return of 5.61% which doesn’t sound great at first. But, as Poland’s growth has accelerated, so too has the ETF pegged to it.

Over the past 10 years, EPOL has seen an average return of 9.79%. Over the past 5 years it has returned 16.8%, beating the average S&P 500 growth of about 10% annually. Over the past three years it has averaged a 37.1% return, and in the past year it returned 76.25%.

The ETF holds 33 of Poland’s top companies, including Powszechna Kasa Oszczednosci Bank Polski Spólka Akcyjna (PKO.WA), a major bank in Poland and Europe more broadly which is the biggest position in the ETF at 14.59%.

It also holds Orlen S.A. (PKN.WA), which is an oil refinery and natural gas trader that makes up 13% of EPOL’s holdings. There’s also Allegro S.A. (ALE.WA), which is akin to a Polish Amazon (AMZN). And I already mentioned CD Projekt, which makes up 3% of the ETF’s holdings.

And that brings me to the difficult part of this article which is the comparison. Because there really aren’t any other ETFs focused on Poland in the way EPOL is. Aside from it, the ETF with the most weighting in the Polish economy is the Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF (FRDM). Polish stocks make up about 15% of its holdings.

But seeing as Poland is about to join the G20 I don’t think it really counts as an “emerging economy” anymore. Poland has emerged, and EPOL is the only way for an American to invest in it without going through the difficulties of registering to trade on the Warsaw exchange in Poland.

And, while EPOL and FRDM have a comparable 1-year return of 76% to 71%, EPOL wins out in the longer term with its 37.1% 3-year average return dwarfing FRDM’s 28.7%.

FRDM is cheaper to hold with an expense ratio of 0.49% to EPOL’s 0.59% but its performance is worse and it’s not focused on Poland so there’s a chance the fund will ditch its Polish stocks and move that money into another economy.

As for EPOL itself, the ETF has $638 million in assets under management and with a 3-month average volume of 479,472 it should be plenty liquid. Now, on to what I think it’s going to do this year.

Due to the fund’s growing average annual returns, I’m comfortable with a projection of about $60 by the year’s end, which would be a 71% increase over the $35.19 EPOL was trading at when 2026 began and a repeat of 2025’s performance.

The ETF is up 2.4% year-to-date but it was up 10% before the start of the current conflict between Iran, Israel, and the United States which caused a contraction in markets the world over.

Now, here’s why I think EPOL can hit that figure.

The Catalyst: The South Korea of the West

It only took South Korea 56 years to transform from an agrarian economy to the 11th largest economy in the world by GDP, a title it claimed in 2016. Since then, as the country’s growth has slowed it has fallen to number 14 by GDP. Still, it remains one of the most dynamic economies in the world.

Poland is on track to do something similar.

The country’s GDP crossed the $1 trillion mark in September 2025 and it’s now the 20th largest economy in the world. In the 35 years since the fall of communism, Poland has eclipsed the economies of Sweden, Switzerland, and Taiwan.

If you adjust for purchasing power, Poland’s GDP per capita is set to pass Japan’s this year.

And, with a serious dispute in the G20 between the United States and South Africa, Poland has an opportunity to be promoted from observer status to a full member of the G20. And, after the U.S. snubbed South Africa at 2025’s G20 meeting in Johannesburg, Washington has invited Poland to the 2026 G20 summit in Miami while not inviting South Africa.

If Poland becomes a fully-fledged member of the G20, Warsaw will gain greater say in the global economy which is likely to only accelerate the country’s economic growth.

Further, Poland is very open to foreign investment and is set to be the largest recipient of EU funding from 2028-2034. And it has spent the money it’s already received very well, modernizing its infrastructure and stimulating its industries.

The country is already the 6th largest economy in the EU, and the seventh in Europe. And, due to its being part of the EU, it has easy access to the bloc’s roughly half-a-billion people. Its largest export markets. Germany, Czechia, France, and the U.K. are all close by and Poland has become the United States’ 36th largest export market so it’s already well-integrated into the global market.

Poland’s economic development has been smart and well-managed and it’s now time for the country to join the rest of the world’s major economies. If it took Poland less than 40 years to go from Soviet Satellite to the G20, I’m incredibly bullish on what Poland will do with the next 40 years.

There are risks though, as there are with any investment, and the biggest one for Poland is hardly unique to it.

The Risks: Empty Factories and Troublesome Neighbors

Poland has a low birth rate and, like the entire rest of the world, it’s falling. The average birth rate in the European Union has fallen to 1.4 children per woman and Poland is below that number at 1.2.

It’s better off than China at 1 even and almost double South Korea at 0.7, but it is a problem that will likely see Poland’s population decline from 38 million today to about 30 million in 2060. And, given that birthrates globally are falling rapidly, any opening of immigration policy would at best be a band aid on the problem.

The highest birthrate in the world is Somalia but even theirs has fallen from 7 children per woman to 6 from 2000 to 2023. A glance at the Worldbank’s statistics shows there is virtually no country that has a growing birthrate anywhere in the world.

So, yes, Poland’s declining birthrate is a problem but it’s a problem the entire world is going to have to face barring another baby boom like we saw in the wake of World War II.

However, a demographic trend like that takes decades to play out and Poland has managed incredible economic growth despite remaining at about 38 million people since the fall of communism so I’m no more worried about a declining birthrate there than I am for pretty much any other country.

Another less likely risk is geopolitical. Poland has always been sandwiched between Russia and Germany but it has so economically integrated itself with Germany that I find any conflict between the two extremely unlikely.

And Russia, despite its aggression in Ukraine, has struggled to fight a war wherein it has massive advantages in industrial capacity, population, armor, and air support. So, while having Russia as a neighbor is a risk, I don’t think Poland has too much to worry about on its eastern flank for the foreseeable future. Especially considering that Russia’s casualties from the war in Ukraine have been extremely high.

Hard numbers are difficult to find but estimates of Russian casualties range from 1.2 million to 1.8 million with 325,000 dead. So, regardless of how the war ends, it will likely take Russia years to rebuild its military.

So, Poland has a serious opportunity here to emerge as an economy to rival the likes of France, Britain, or even Germany. I think the growth is just getting started and EPOL is the best option Americans have to play it.

The Verdict: Poland is Far From Lost

Poland is shaping up to be Europe’s own South Korea or Japan. It’s a country that has emerged from the tragedies of the 20th century as a strong, dynamic, and growing economy and an increasingly important voice in world affairs.

Since the fall of communism, Poland has only experienced one year of economic contraction and it consistently outpaces every other country in Europe in terms of economic growth.

The country’s companies are beginning to break out of Poland’s domestic market and that domestic market is one of the strongest in Europe.

History doesn’t repeat but it does rhyme, and Poland is shaping up to be one of the greatest economic success stories of the 21st century just like Japan and South Korea were in the 20th.

And iShares MSCI Poland ETF is one of the best ways to play it. It’s a buy from me.

data.title: Corning: Secular AI Infrastructure Growth Collides With A Premium Valuation
data.description:
This is my first coverage of Corning (GLW). In this article, I will discuss Corning’s business, and how the macro situation will impact them, the recent Q4 earnings results, and the valuation. I am issuing a "Hold" rating on the stock, as I believe the future potential is largely priced into the current stock price, leaving little margin of safety. I should also note that I really like their business and that the underlying secular trends will provide strong tailwinds for the company. As a consequence, if we get a pullback, I would turn bullish and initiate the position.

Macro Trends

We are currently seeing a boom in everything related to AI. Data center construction is on the rise, with data center vacancies reaching a record low of 1.4% at year-end, according to CBRE. This, in my opinion, completely negates the bearish theory that we are at the peak of the AI boom. As long as vacancy rates are low, data center construction companies will keep on building, providing further demand for Corning’s products. I also discussed these trends in my recent articles about Crypto Miners pivoting to the HPC sector; if you are interested in learning more about these trends, I advise reading these articles.

To provide further evidence of continued growth, we need to look at the guided CapEx spend of the hyperscalers. According to CreditSights, the top 5 hyperscalers are projected to spend $750 billion in 2026, which represents 67% growth YoY and is driven by AI infrastructure investments. This will inevitably lead to further purchases of Corning’s AI-related products, like fiber optic cable.

In general, the macro trends give me a strong confidence that Corning’s growth momentum will continue.

Q4 Earnings Results

Corning reported Q4 earnings in January. They announced record revenue of approx. $4.2 billion, growing approx. 20%, with Q4 core sales rising 14% YoY. The company reached a record GAAP operating margin of 16.5%, with the core (non-GAAP) operating margin reaching 20.2%. This discrepancy between core (non-GAAP) and GAAP results is, as the company stated, due to "non-cash, mark-to-market adjustments associated with the company’s translated earnings contracts and foreign-denominated debt as well as constant currency adjustments." Note that the management mentioned in the earnings call that ''we are not changing our operating margin target at this time.'' I will model my valuation accordingly, with a minimal margin increase in the future, which can change based on the management updates in the upcoming earnings releases.

I also want to mention that 14% growth in their core sales growth seems low at first when compared to the increases in CapEx hyperscalers are spending on AI, and one could conclude that Corning is losing market share. Such a conclusion is not correct, as Corning is operating in multiple verticals; the Display segment contracted 2% YoY, the Specialty segment grew 6% YoY, and the Automotive segment contracted 1% YoY. These slow-moving segments dilute the revenue growth in the Optical Communications segment.

What gives me additional confidence in further growth are the revisions of the Springboard Initiative goals. Launched in Q4 2023, the plan was changed and now adds $11 billion in incremental annualized sales by the end of 2028, up from the original $8 billion plan, and adds $6.5 billion in incremental annualized sales by the end of 2026, up from the previous $6 billion plan. Due to the secular trends discussed above, I am highly confident the company will raise their revenue targets again.

Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses

Another vertical I should talk about is Hemlock, which is the fastest-growing segment with 62% revenue growth in Q4 YoY. Corning owns a majority stake in Hemlock Semiconductor, which is a domestic manufacturer of pure polysilicon. Hemlock's polysilicon is used as a base material for both crystalline silicon solar cells and silicon wafers. With the US slowly decoupling from China and bringing chip manufacturing from Taiwan to the US, I believe both solar and semiconductor polysilicon are poised to become an important contributor to Corning’s bottom line.

Valuation

Although I am really optimistic about Corning’s business, the current valuation makes me a bit worried. A part of the premium value can be justified by the incredible moat the company has. Remember, this is a capital-intensive business, and building a fiber optics production plant takes a lot of time and cash.

Still, by all metrics, the company is expensive. Management is projecting a 15% core sales growth for the first quarter of 2026 while not giving further margin expansion targets. In my opinion, the fair PE multiple would be 25x, possibly 35x if we argue the company has a strong moat. The current non-GAAP PE multiple is 57x. Based on this metric, the company is currently overvalued, but let's try to calculate if they will grow into such a valuation.

Due to the secular trends I discussed, I estimate that Corning will grow their core revenues at 15% (which is the same as Q1 guided growth) until 2030 before slowing down to low double-digits; say 11%. I estimate that the core (non-GAAP) operating margin will moderately increase from the current 20% to 25%. Interest and tax expenses are currently 5% of revenues. I am estimating they will remain at 5%, making the 2030 net income margin 20%. Accordingly, net income would amount to $6.6 billion.

The final fair PE multiple at 11% growth should, in my opinion, be around 20-25x, considering Corning’s moat. Thus, the fair market cap in 2030 would be $132 billion to $165 billion, compared to the current market cap of approx. $124 billion. This would represent a 6.5% and 33% upside, respectively.

Conclusion and Plan

Based on my calculations in the Valuation chapter, I believe investing in the stock at the current price provides an investor a minimal margin of safety, as the company must have perfect execution to grow into the current valuation, while providing limited upside. Any potential recessions, slowdown in demand for fiber optics, or potential competition will further decrease the potential upside. In my opinion, it is better to wait for a correction before initializing a position.

data.title: Forget Iran, South Korea Is The Real Threat To Markets
data.description:
Thesis Summary

While everyone is looking at Iran, the KOSPI (KOSPI) has crashed 20% in the last two days.

That’s the index tracking the South Korean markets, and it has big repercussions for U.S. investors.

South Korea sits at the heart of the AI hardware ecosystem, and as this trade unwinds, the repercussions could quickly spread to the West too.

So, should investors be worried, or is it perhaps time to buy the dip in South Korean stocks?

What Actually Happened

The sell-off began when the KOSPI dropped more than 12% in a single session, followed by another brutal decline the next day. Trading was halted multiple times as this sudden surge in volatility triggered circuit breakers.

To put this into perspective, South Korean stocks (EWY) have more than doubled in the last year.

This is because a large part of the index is made up of AI hardware stocks like Samsung Electronics and SK hynix. And these stocks have also led the decline.

But what triggered this sell-off?

I can think of two key catalysts.

  • Energy exposure

  • Leverage

Let’s break this down below.

The Energy Shock

South Korea is, with good reason, regarded as one of the most technologically advanced countries in the world.

And yet, technology isn't everything, and South Korea produces almost none of its own energy.

The country is, in fact, one of the largest importers of oil and LNG in the world.

This is undeniably a point of weakness. South Korea’s industry is highly sensitive to changes in the oil and energy market, and there’s not much it can do about it.

As the conflict in the Middle East escalated, Asian LNG prices surged to $25.40 per million BTU, doubling in just a week.

Meanwhile, Qatar, one of Korea’s key suppliers, suspended some output, tightening supply even further.

That’s a major problem for a country whose entire economic model is built on exporting manufactured goods.

Higher energy prices would eventually lead to tighter margins and potentially even weaken the currency.

This has forced investors to reassess their positions in Korea, which likely triggered the initial selling.

The Leverage Problem

But the real accelerant was leverage.

South Korea has one of the most aggressive retail trading cultures in the world. Margin debt has surged in recent years, reaching roughly 32 trillion won, $22 billion, earlier this year.

The trade worked great for some time, driven by chip stocks, and this prompted more leverage, creating a self-feeding loop.

But when a market becomes crowded and leveraged, any excuse can trigger a big sell-off, and that’s exactly what happened here.

Once the first margin calls hit, forced selling began, and so on and so forth.

Why This Matters For U.S. Markets

At first glance, a crash in South Korea might seem like a localized issue, but to anyone who has been following the investing world over the last two years, it's easy to see the spillover effects.

South Korea is a critical player in the global semiconductor supply chain, as its companies supply components used by virtually every major AI infrastructure player.

That includes an extensive list of companies listed on the U.S. stock market

Obviously, chip makers like Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are directly affected.

But there are also many others that could suffer from South Korea's problems, like Apple (AAPL) or even Microsoft (MSFT).

In fact, it would almost be harder to list the companies that wouldn't be affected by disruptions in South Korea's supply chain.

In today’s tech-heavy world, Korean companies underpin many of the global giants based in the U.S. In turn, these companies make up a very large part of the S&P 500.

On top of that, South Korean investors are heavy participants in global equities, particularly U.S. tech.

If they face margin calls at home, they may need to sell foreign holdings to raise cash.

It’s similar in a way to the unwinding of the Japan carry trade, which also rocked markets in the U.S. last year.

In fact, Korean, Japanese, and U.S. stocks now hold a very tight correlation.

Oil Is Still The Real Story

Zooming out, the deeper message here isn’t about Korea specifically.

It’s about energy, something I also highlighted in my recent Iran piece.

Industrial powerhouses like South Korea are especially sensitive to price shocks.

If oil and gas prices spike for a sustained period, it creates a chain reaction that leads to higher production costs, currency weakness, margin compression, and a de-risking event in equity markets.

That’s the dynamic we’re starting to see.

What Investors Should Watch Next

The outcome of this is ultimately down to what happens with energy, and that also depends on how long the war lasts.

As I write this, markets are back in the green and oil is down, in hopes that the U.S. government will at least do its best to protect the oil supply.

But the fundamental story, as far as I see it, continues to be quite bullish. The AI cycle is still intact, and memory demand is still strong.

With leverage and positioning now cleared out, I think this looks like a chance to buy the dip and enjoy a reversal.

But if the selling continues, the ripple effects could extend far beyond Seoul.

data.title: Wix.com: No AI Apocalypse In Sight, Only Tailwinds (Upgrade)
data.description:
One of the most incredible things about the stock market in 2026 is how quickly the AI narrative turned from a tailwind to a headwind for stocks. The "SaaSpocalypse" headlines have dominated trading this year and decimated software stocks, with seemingly no end in sight.

This gives investors with strong stomachs who prioritize entering at good value a great opportunity to buy stocks like Wix.com (WIX). Wix, the web hosting and web creation platform, has seen its revenue and bookings growth pull well ahead of peers, suggesting that Wix is a net winner in the AI race. And yet investors have been dumping the stock as if it were already a legacy play.

Over the past year, Wix has lost about 60% of its value: however, a strong "beat and raise" in Q4, alongside commentary on the rapid growth in the company's AI/vibe coding subsidiary, Base44, have helped spark a post-earnings rally. On top of a boosted buyback program, I believe this is the beginning of a much broader rebound for the company.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Wix in December, when the stock was trading closer to $95 per share. While I acknowledge that my "Buy" call was ill-timed, I have no less conviction in the stock today even after its recent correction. I continue to emphasize that all the data coming out of Wix's most recent earnings supports the narrative that AI is a huge net tailwind, not a headwind for the company. Amid the striking value opportunity we have here, I'm bumping up Wix to a "Strong Buy" rating, representing my highest level of conviction that I reserve for very few stocks.

Deep value amid vibrant and growing business, massive buybacks

The number-one reason driving my conviction on Wix is the fact that investors already have a highly de-risked entry point in the stock, thanks to the sharp slide over the past year that has been completely incommensurate with actual business fundamentals. This is especially true when we consider the fact that Wix's growth trends are beginning to diverge notably from other web hosting platforms.

At current share prices near $84, Wix trades at a market cap of $4.57 billion. Netting off the $1.65 billion of cash and $1.13 billion of convertible debt on Wix's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $4.05 billion.

For FY26, Wix is guiding to rather vague, verbal targets calling for mid-teens revenue/bookings growth and low-to-mid-20s FCF margins.

If we take a conservative interpretation of this guidance language and assume 15% revenue growth and 23% FCF margins (which is already substantially reduced from a 29% margin in FY25, which accounts for potential margin compression as AI/Base44 costs ramp up), Wix would hit $2.29 billion in revenue and $527 million in FCF.

This positions the stock's valuation at multiples of:

  • 1.8x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 7.7x EV/FY26 FCF

We note not only that these are tremendously low valuation multiples for a company expecting mid-teens top-line growth at healthy gross margins, but the company is also leveraging its low valuation to execute a massive share buyback. The company is planning to sink $2 billion into share buybacks this year (representing 44% of the company's current market cap), which reflects the huge conviction that management has in its AI opportunity and the fact that the stock is currently heavily mispriced.

We note that Wix's current balance sheet cash ($1.65 billion), plus ongoing ~$0.5+ billion in free cash flow, offers plenty of cash firepower to support this ambitious repurchase plan.

Q4 download

Next, let's continue to put substance behind the notion that AI is absolutely not a tailwind to Wix's business. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Wix's revenue grew 14% y/y to $524.3 million, not at all decelerating from a 14% pace in Q3. Meanwhile, we note that the company's bookings (which is the best forward-looking indicator of Wix's demand profile) grew 15% y/y to $535 million, outpacing revenue growth in both growth and nominal terms and accelerating slightly over 14% growth in Q3.

The next major publicly traded website builder and hosting platform is GoDaddy (GDDY). In the same quarter, GoDaddy achieved just 5% bookings growth and 7% revenue growth. In other words, Wix is doubling its next major rival's revenue growth rate and tripling its bookings growth.

One of the core ways in which Wix is differentiating itself is through Base44, its vibe coding startup. The company notes that it just unveiled plenty of new capabilities for Base44, enabling native mobile app development as well as adding monetization and payments capabilities. Wix noted that Base44 ended Q4/FY25 at a $59 million ARR, but "recently" mid-quarter in Q1 (as of the date of its earnings release, which was in early March), the platform hit $100 million in ARR. The scale of that growth is staggering: though at $100 million in annual revenue, Base44 is only ~5% of Wix's overall revenue, the fact that it's growing at nearly a 2x pace on a sequential quarter-over-quarter basis is truly remarkable. And again, it offers solid proof that AI is a net tailwind for Wix, not a headwind.

Speaking to Base44's traction on the Q4 earnings call, CEO Avishai Abrahami commented as follows:

The second new pillar of our strategy is Base44, our leading vibe coding platform that expands our reach into the vast world of software creation and significantly grows our TAM. We are equally ambitious in this new world as we are in the world of website creation through innovation and strong marketing execution, Base44's user base is scaling rapidly. Today, the number of new users joining Base44 is nearly 2/3 of the number of new users joining Wix. This is an indicator of the platform's accelerating momentum and highlights the opportunity ahead to empower a massive and compounding cohort of users to build whatever they want online. Just 1 year after Maor founded the company and 9 months after our acquisition, Base44 recently reached approximately $100 million of ARR, placing it among the fastest-growing software platforms in history.

While Base44 is already emerging as a top platform to build lightweight personal projects, we're seeing adoption from a growing community of businesses and enterprise-sized organizations, too. Companies in the tech, banking and healthcare industries as well as government organizations and nonprofits are using Base44 to build customized software solutions. We are seeing users develop their own CRM capabilities, product and project management tools, ERP systems, workflow automation frameworks and financial reporting applications. Importantly, this momentum and growth is completely organic with no sales team at Base44 today, self-propelled adoption by enterprise-size organizations demonstrates the strength of the platform as well as our successful marketing execution.

Where the risk comes in, however, is on the company's margin profile. Pro forma gross margins fell 2 points y/y to 68% in Q4, driven by higher AI costs and Base44 usage. In addition, supporting Base44's R&D and sales needs also drove spending higher, with total opex rising 52% y/y on a GAAP basis to $425.6 million.

As a result, as shown in the chart below, Wix's pro forma operating income fell 19% y/y to $81.2 million, with margins of 15%, losing 7 points y/y.

Full-year free cash flow of $573 million, which included $32.1 million of costs to fund the company's new headquarters buildout, grew 20% y/y.

Risks and key takeaways

To me, the biggest risk that Wix faces is an inability to achieve scaling margins in the company's vibe coding/Base44 business. AI companies are in a "growth at all costs" and market share grab mentality, but the reality is often that services are priced below cost. The environment is hugely competitor-dependent, and we do worry that if Base44 continues to face deeper-pocketed competitors like Anthropic that can afford to price services below cost for longer, the company may end up losing the market share wars.

That said, I think this risk is already more than priced into Wix's stock at ~8x forward FCF (especially since forward guidance already bakes in operating margin and FCF compression to account for these AI cost dynamics). Stay long here and keep riding the post-earnings rally higher.

data.title: Okta Lost The Cybersecurity Plot (Q4 Review)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Okta (OKTA), I downgraded the stock to a "hold," as management was repeatedly failing to demonstrate meaningful signs of revenue acceleration, despite management’s optimism towards their product innovation roadmap that they believed would benefit from industry tailwinds around securing agents and non-human identities.

The company reported their Q4 FY26 earnings yesterday, and unfortunately, management failed to deliver once again.

While they beat their revenue and non-GAAP operating margin guidance for the quarter, forward guidance shows no sign of inflection.

On the contrary.

Management now expects forward revenues to slow compared to their FY26 levels with a slight deterioration in margins.

This is not what "benefiting from industry tailwinds" looks like.

With no improvement in their Net Retention Rate for four consecutive quarters now, I believe that the company continues to face sluggish growth in new customer additions, upsells, and renewals and is thus slowly fading into irrelevance.

The only way out of this misery would be a potential takeover, especially as it has solid product leadership in Identity and Access Management, along with a strong balance sheet.

Unfortunately, with its decelerating revenue momentum along with its past history of security incidents, potential buyers may be hesitant.

Until then, we can expect the stock to remain pressured, and I will therefore downgrade it to a "sell."

Better-than-expected Performance for Both Top and Bottom Line

Okta just reported its Q4 FY26 earnings, where both revenue and cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations) continued to slow from the previous quarter, growing 11% and 12% YoY, vs. management’s guidance of 10% and 9%, respectively.

The outperformance in results compared to management’s guidance was likely driven by their $100K+ ACV (Annual Contract Value) customers, which continued to grow more or less at a steady pace compared to the previous quarter, now representing over 80% of total ACV. At the same time, their total base of $1M+ ACV grew at an accelerated pace of 16% to 545 customers, which now represents $1B in total ACV.

In terms of their platform contribution, Workforce Identity ACV grew 10% YoY, representing 59% of total ACV, while Customer Identity ACV grew 12% YoY, contributing the remaining 41% of total ACV. As I have explained in my previous posts, Okta Workforce is directed towards large enterprises and IT teams with use cases that include employee access, workflows, and compliance across cloud and on-premise applications. Meanwhile, their Customer Identity platform is directed towards developers and product teams.

To reiterate, Okta offers two primary platforms that include Okta Workforce and Okta Customer Identity, which are powered by Auth0 technology. Okta Workforce is directed towards large enterprises and IT teams with use cases that include employee access, workflows, and compliance across cloud and on-premises applications. Meanwhile, the Okta Customer Identity platform powered by Auth0 is directed towards developers and product teams that are building user-facing/customer applications that require a secure and customizable authentication and authorization experience for their users who are accessing the service. Last quarter, they also launched Auth0 for AI agents to help their customers build secure AI agents across their B2B, B2C, and internal app ecosystems.

As Okta doubles down on their product innovation roadmap, what is interesting to note is that their margins haven’t taken a beating. During the quarter, Okta continued to expand their total gross margin to 82.1%. The same is true for their non-GAAP operating margins, which continued to expand both on a sequential and year-over-year basis to 26.5%, 150 basis points higher than what management had guided for.

No Inflection in Sight

However, forward guidance continues to remain lackluster, with revenue and cRPO projected to slow to 9% and 10%, respectively, in Q1 FY27. For the full year FY27, management expects revenue to grow just 9% YoY to $3.18B (at the midpoint), which would mark a deceleration on a YoY basis.

We can also see this playing out in the Net Retention figures, which have now remained steady at 106% for the last four consecutive quarters. With no signs of an inflection higher, this indicates that the pace of new customer additions, upsells, and/or renewals continues to remain sluggish.

On top of that, Okta seems to be reaching a plateau with its operating leverage, especially as revenue growth fails to pick up momentum. For the full year FY27, management is projecting non-GAAP operating margins at 25-26%, compared to 26% in FY26, while free cash flow margin is also expected to head lower in the 27-28% range, compared to 30% in FY26.

What Can Halt Its Descent to Irrelevance

Looking forward, consensus estimates are pointing to Okta growing in the high single-digit range over the next three years to roughly $3.75B by FY29. From a profitability standpoint, its earnings per share are expected to grow at a slightly faster rate than revenue growth expectations, with analysts expecting roughly $4.65 in earnings per share by FY29.

The stock is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP PE ratio of 20x, which is expected to further compress to 16.3x when taking FY29 earnings per share estimates into account.

Sure, the stock is “cheap” by traditional valuation methods. But, then again, with enterprises increasingly consolidating towards fully integrated cybersecurity platforms, the inflection would be hard to come by for Okta at this stage. Without a meaningful inflection in their revenue growth, the stock will just slowly fade into irrelevance, which is already happening at the moment.

What can save Okta at this point?

A potential takeover perhaps.

We saw that with Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) acquiring Okta’s nearest competitor CyberArk to deepen their platformization strategy. But then again, CyberArk had been growing its revenues at a significantly faster pace than Okta’s.

When it comes to Okta, it has several characteristics of a potential takeover candidate. First, its product leadership in Identity and Access Management is strong, along with a rock-solid balance sheet. Not to mention, its forward PE ratio is at its lowest level in years and below industry averages.

However, its decelerating growth, along with past security incidents, may be keeping potential buyers at bay for now.

My Final Verdict and Conclusions

With Okta failing to show any signs of revenue inflection despite its product innovation and Agentic AI as a tailwind, the company continues to fade into irrelevance.

The situation is probably getting further aggravated amid ongoing vendor consolidation as enterprises move away from point solutions towards fully integrated platforms to securely deploy AI at scale.

Therefore, without any compelling event, such as a potential takeover, investor sentiment is unlikely to improve on Okta even though the company has a rock-solid balance sheet and is trading at its lowest PE ratio in over a year.

data.title: Oil Spike May Scramble The Fed's Policy Script As War In Iran Continues
data.description:
By James Picerno

The Federal Reserve was already facing a delicate transition as Kevin Warsh prepares to take over in May, pending Senate confirmation, when Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s term ends. The war in Iran adds another layer of uncertainty to an already fraught policy environment.

Energy prices have spiked since the US and Israel attacked Iran on Saturday, a conflict that’s slowed oil exports to a crawl through the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly one-fifth of the world’s seaborne oil trade moved through this strategic chokepoint last year. If this narrow waterway remains closed for an extended period, it raises the possibility that inflation could heat up and force the Fed to respond - if not by raising interest rates, then by delaying rate cuts for longer than recently expected.

Fed funds futures are now downplaying the odds of a rate cut in June. Before the war, this was considered the month when the central bank would start cutting again, extending last year’s run of easing. But the market is rethinking that logic and is now pricing in a moderately high probability that the Fed leaves rates steady in June, pushing out the expected date for the next cut to July or possibly September at the earliest.

The concern is that if energy costs remain elevated, the higher prices of crude oil, gasoline, and natural gas will soon feed through to the broader economy, delivering a so-called negative supply shock. Standard economic theory, along with the historical record, suggests that the higher prices will slow growth and lift inflation to some degree.

Energy prices are expected to fall once the conflict is over, but US and Israeli officials say the war could run for weeks. A related concern is that the widening damage to energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf region from Iran’s retaliatory attacks will take time to repair - a bottleneck that will keep supplies lower for longer.

Geopolitics vs. monetary policy

The main concern for the Fed is how, or if, to respond to higher energy costs. To the extent that oil drives headline inflation higher, the shift presents an especially difficult challenge for monetary policy, since interest rates have no direct effect on the supply of crude. That’s one reason why the Fed and other central banks tend to target core measures of inflation, which strip out energy and food costs - a subset of pricing over which policy has substantial influence.

The Fed’s job will be even tougher because deciding if the war has raised inflation in a meaningful and persistent degree will take time. Next week’s scheduled release of consumer price inflation for February, for instance, is already irrelevant in the current environment.

TMC Research’s Fed Model continues to indicate that policy remains modestly tight, but the hawkish spread of the effective Fed funds rate over our estimated neutral rate is fading. As recently as late January, the Fed’s target rate was more than 60 basis points above this proxy for estimating an equilibrium level. The premium (implying a moderately hawkish bias) has been sliding since, dropping to under 30 basis points on Tuesday (March 3). In other words, Fed policy is approaching a neutral bias, and looks set to continue into dovish terrain - a trend that may quickly become inappropriate if inflation heats up.

Hikes Off the Table - For Now

Rate hikes are still unlikely, based on market expectations for inflation. The Treasury market’s 5-year breakeven inflation rate (nominal less inflation-indexed yield) was just below 2.5% yesterday (March 4), a middling level versus recent history for this bond market inflation gauge.

The 1-month Treasury yield appears to be pricing in no change in the target rate, as it remains at the upper half of the policy channel. The calculus would change if the 1-month yield - currently at 3.75% - decisively breaks above the channel’s upper range, marked in the chart below by the Fed’s discount window primary rate, which is also at 3.75%. In that case, the shift would signal that the market is starting to factor in the possibility of a rate hike.

This macro mix would be hard enough for any new Fed chairman, but it may be especially challenging for Warsh, who has supported President Trump’s public calls for lower interest rates, partly on the view that an artificial intelligence-driven productivity boom will boost economic growth without raising inflation.

Whatever the merits of that forecast (and there’s ongoing debate on the topic), the Iran war may rewrite the script for Warsh’s policy plans well before he arrives at the Eccles Building on Constitution Avenue.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: B2Gold: Strong Growth Is Overshadowed By The Bearish Technical Outlook
data.description:
I'm providing a post-earnings update for B2Gold (BTG) for Q4 2025. BTG reported strong revenue growth as the gross margin continued to grow. The stock is still trading at a bargain valuation. The company's expected growth for 2026 looks strong. My only concern is that the stock has formed a bearish technical set-up, which typically results in a stock correction.

B2Gold has been a standout performing stock as it is up 19% since my last update was written in September 2025. The stock is up 62% since I first initiated coverage on B2Gold in July 2025. I expect the stock to continue to perform well in 2026 if the underlying price of gold and the sentiment towards the industry remains strong. However, a shorter-term correction looks likely for BTG's stock and the underlying price of gold.

Insights From the Q4 2025 Earnings Report

The Q4 2025 earnings report provides multiple insights into B2Gold's likely growth for 2026.

The company is forecasting to produce 820,000 to 970,000 ounces in 2026. The projection is lower for 2026 due to the planned step-down of Otjikoto after the completion of the open pit mining in Q4 2025. It is also due to lower production at the Fekola mine due to the continuation of the stripping of Phase 8 in the Fekola pit. The lower production is expected to be partially offset by the continued ramp-up of the Goose mine. BTG stated that all of their operations are performing above expectations so far in 2026.

One key possible growth driver for 2026 is that B2Gold expects to obtain the permit from Mali for Fekola regional as early as Q1 with production beginning in the 2nd half of 2026. BTG is projecting Fekola Regional to produce 60,000 to 80,000 ounces in 2026. Obtaining the permit for Fekola can be a long-term growth driver for the company in 2026 and beyond.

The company expects the Goose mine to ramp up throughout the year. Expectations are for Goose's crushing circuit to produce 3,200 tonnes per day after some modifications to improve performance. BTG is studying improvements to get the crushing circuit up to an average of 4,000 tonnes per day. Those studies are expected to be finalized in the 1st half of 2026.

BTG expects another year of consistent operations at Masbate in 2026. This follows production at Masbate that exceeded the company's expectations with 2 years in a row of record results in 2024 & 2025. BTG expects Otjikoto to produce less in 2026 as the mine transitions to processing only the Wolfshag underground ore, supplemented by low-grade stockpiles. The company is developing the Antelope deposit, which BTG believes can get Otjikoto to produce an average of 110,000 ounces from 2029 to 2032.

B2Gold continues to return capital to shareholders in the form of stock repurchases. BTG repurchased 2 million shares worth $10 million in 2025. The company also repurchased another 5 million shares worth about $24 million so far in 2026. BTG plans to continue the repurchases for the remainder of 2026. These repurchases reduce the amount of shares outstanding, making existing shares more valuable for stockholders.

Overall, B2Gold is displaying multiple solid potential growth drivers. These growth drivers should help the company to continue to grow revenue and earnings at a strong pace in 2026.

Technical Perspective

The main concern that I have for B2Gold is its bearish technical set-up.

B2Gold's daily chart above shows the stock in a bearish divergence. This is indicated as the stock price made a higher high from the end of January to the end of February. At the same time, the RSI made a lower high. The last time a bearish divergence like this occurred was in October. The October divergence resulted in the stock dropping from just below $6 down to $3.70.

I don't know if the stock will decline by such a drastic amount this time. I do think that the stock could experience a significant profit-taking pullback with this bearish-looking set-up. A pullback recently began over the past few days.

The MACD just made a bearish move. This is indicated as the blue MACD line crossed below the red signal line while the histogram bars turned from green to red. This suggests that the momentum shifted from positive to negative. This momentum shift could lead to a longer decline for BTG's stock.

It is important to note that any pullback in the stock would probably be shallow if the price of gold remains strong. B2Gold's stock tends to correlate well with the underlying price of gold.

The chart above displays the monthly chart of the underlying price of gold. B2Gold's stock tends to correlate well with the price of gold. So, it is important to understand where the price of the commodity is likely to go.

The price of gold reached an extreme overbought level on the monthly chart as indicated in the mid-90s on the RSI. Levels this high on the RSI are typically unsustainable. The price recently began to decline so far in March as the RSI dropped from the mid-90s to the low 90s.

The MACD is indicating waning momentum so far in March as the histogram bars turned from dark green to light green. This could be the beginning of a larger correction for the price of gold. If this occurs, it would likely negatively impact BTG's stock.

Valuation

B2Gold is still trading at a bargain valuation. The stock is trading at 7.5x expected EPS of $0.73 for 2026. This is significantly below the sector median forward PE of 16x. I also like to use the PEG ratio for growth companies like BTG, as it takes multiple years of growth into account. BTG has a low PEG ratio of 0.56. This is also below the sector median PEG of 1.2. The growth stocks that I cover tend to perform well when the PEG is below two. So, when I see a strong growth stock with a PEG below one, that really shows a bargain.

The expected EPS of $0.73 for 2026 would be about 60% growth over 2025. This can be achieved with analysts' expected revenue growth of 37% to $4.2 billion for 2026. Then, earnings can get a boost from the margin expansion that BTG has been achieving. The gross margin increased from 56.5% in 2024 to 64.4% in 2025. The operating margin increased from 30% to 36.8%, and the net income margin increased from a negative (33%) to 13% over the same time.

B2Gold's low valuation leaves plenty of room to the upside for the stock due to the potential for PE expansion. At this low level, the stock has a good chance to increase at a rate that exceeds BTG's earnings growth.

Risks for the Stock

Since B2Gold's stock is highly correlated to the price of gold, sharp drops in the price of gold would likely result in sharp declines in BTG's stock. Lower gold prices would lead to lower revenue for BTG, as the company would earn less from each ounce of gold sold. The price of gold is currently at extreme overbought levels, which may not be sustainable in the near term. This could lead to a sharp correction for the price of gold and B2Gold's stock.

An unexpected delay in obtaining the Fekola Regional permit from Mali could lead to a sell-off for the stock. A long delay would lower BTG's production estimates for Fekola Regional for 2026, leading to less revenue & earnings for the year.

B2Gold faces geopolitical, legal, and regulatory risks in the countries where it operates. This includes Mali, Canada, Namibia, the Philippines, and Colombia. These countries could impose new laws and regulations, which could increase BTG's costs of doing business or make it more difficult to expand production.

The recent military actions between the U.S./Israel, and Iran could trigger a sell-off for the broader market which could negatively impact B2Gold.

B2Gold's Investment Outlook

B2Gold looks great from a growth perspective through at least 2026. The ramp-up of the Goose mine and obtaining the permit at Fekola Regional can be strong growth drivers this year. The stock's valuation remains attractive, which, along with BTG's strong earnings growth, has the potential to help drive the stock for strong above-average long-term gains.

B2Gold does face some short-term uncertainties. The military actions between the U.S./Israel and Iran could lead to a sell-off for the market and BTG's stock. Even without this event occurring, the stock's technical level suggests the potential for a profit-taking pullback. Investors should watch to see how the market reacts to this recent news to re-evaluate their positions. I'm lowering my rating for the stock to 'HOLD' from 'STRONG BUY' due to the technical stock level and the uncertainties regarding the new geopolitical situation.

data.title: Software's Big Sell-Off: Structural Risk Or Narrative Noise?
data.description:
By Lei Qiu, John H. Fogarty, CFA, Kent Hargis, PhD and Thorsten Winkelmann

AI concerns are forcing a rapid reset of software stocks. But disruption rarely unfolds as fast as markets expect.

Software stocks have suffered a rude awakening amid fears that artificial intelligence (AI) will upend the industry. While the outlook is highly uncertain, the indiscriminate market response may be conflating structurally exposed software businesses with more durable companies.

That distinction defines the challenge for investors. AI is raising real doubts about software business models that cannot be ignored. But not all software firms are alike. Some will buckle, while others will survive—and may even thrive—in an AI-driven world.

We believe the market is pricing in disruption as if it’s universal and immediate. Yet technology regime shifts are never so simple. The task now is to identify where disruption is likely to reshape economics and where fears may be running ahead of fundamentals.

Could AI Agents Redefine an Industry?

Since ChatGPT launched, software stocks have been under scrutiny. In recent months, the release of AI coding models Claude Opus 4.5 and Claude Cowork reinforced fears that increasingly capable AI agents—which are being massively deployed (Display)—could commoditize software by reducing development costs and lowering barriers to entry. The MSCI World Software & Services Index tumbled by more than 20% this year through the end of February.

The market is signaling that every software company is at risk—a dramatic change for an industry that has enjoyed positive sentiment for years. Investors have lost conviction in the durability of economic characteristics that historically defined high-quality software businesses, making it nearly impossible to forecast the terminal value of software shares.

The Bear Case: Disruption Could Hit Profit Pools

Profitability concerns are hard to disprove. If AI agents can autonomously execute complex workflows, customers may no longer need full software applications. As a result, traditional user interfaces would lose relevance while switching costs fall and pricing power erodes. More broadly, if AI lowers barriers to entry across large portions of the software ecosystem, supply will rise faster than demand. Increased competition would pressure pricing, margins and returns on capital for incumbents—especially those relying on seat based or feature-driven pricing models.

These risks cannot be dismissed. That said, truly autonomous agents capable of managing complex, regulated, enterprise-scale workflows remain limited today to areas like coding assistance and customer service. So the long-term trajectory is uncertain, and the timing of economic impact remains unclear.

Not All Firms Face the Same Risks

Disruptive impacts won’t be distributed equally across the broad software industry, in our view. Companies providing horizontal application layers—such as customer relations management or workflow automation—are very different to vertical providers of banking systems or retail sales systems, to name a few. Business dynamics differ for infrastructure and cybersecurity firms.

So the key question is: How easy or hard is it to replace certain types of software? Systems of record, for example, aren’t easily displaced because these mission-critical platforms are deeply embedded in organizations with authoritative data sets and compliance frameworks. In contrast, interface apps with low switching costs and limited proprietary data are much easier to replace.

Is the Bear Narrative Running Ahead of Financial Reality?

Nobody really knows where value will accrue across the AI stack (applications, agents, data or infrastructure). This explains why the market has pushed up the discount rate applied to software stocks across the board, which has dragged down valuations (Display). Share prices have fallen despite little sign of operational stress in customer behavior, retention metrics or reported financials.

That said, today’s benign fundamentals may be misleading. Since AI disruption is a structural risk, it wouldn’t show in current company metrics. Firms with solid margins and revenue growth today might look healthy even though pricing and competitive threats loom large.

Another unknown: How will competition intensify among software incumbents as advances in AI coding lower the cost and time required to expand into adjacent categories? This will blur the lines between traditional software domains and erode natural barriers that protect legacy positions.

Financial Transparency Matters: Mind the GAAP

In the search for evidence of AI-driven weakness, accounting transparency will be prized. We expect the market to have less tolerance for non-generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) metrics that adjust earnings by removing stock-based compensation costs.

Structural disruption concerns will compel investors to place greater emphasis on near-term GAAP profitability and traditional valuation metrics. Although GAAP status didn’t protect software stocks in the recent sell-off, we think margin discipline and earnings quality will matter more over time.

Three Things to Watch

The software industry is no stranger to disruption. Well before AI, software was repeatedly redefined by the internet, cloud computing and software as a service (SaaS). To separate winners from losers as AI progresses, investors should focus a playbook on:

  1. Durable competitive moats: Mission-critical software that delivers clear economic value should still command pricing power in a more competitive landscape. Software companies that own authoritative enterprise datasets will be better placed to defend their competitive advantages. And customers will be more reluctant to pay high costs to replace software that is horizontally integrated across an organization.
  2. Clear signs of monetizing AI activity: Software firms must show that AI is helping grow their revenue. Some platforms will benefit if AI agents boost transaction volume (more tickets, approvals and workflows), so look for evidence that AI is bolstering core platforms rather than disintermediating them. In contrast, business models that rely on static seat pricing look vulnerable.
  3. Earnings quality and business model resilience: Businesses with high gross customer retention rates and products embedded in core workflows can defend earnings. Coherent AI strategies have become table stakes for software companies, and proven GAAP-based profitability profiles—which are hard to find right now—will be essential. Distressed valuations could open opportunities in resilient companies that have been tarred with the same brush in the indiscriminate sell-off.

At this juncture in the AI-software story, there’s good reason to be cautious. Structural risk to the software sector is real. History shows that transformative technological disruption takes time to play out, but the magnitude of its impact is often greater than initially expected.

With this in mind, we believe investors shouldn’t be guided by doomsday scenarios or near-term volatility.

In volatile markets, investors tend to shoot first and ask questions later. But that’s not a long-term strategy. As we see it, equity portfolio teams rooted in deep research must ask questions every day to determine where the narrative noise is overdone and how to position appropriately in a software industry facing major disruptive forces. By actively assessing industry developments and the impact on individual companies, disciplined investors can balance patience with conviction while carefully adding exposure in select software stocks that look overly sold and may be primed for recovery.

The authors wish to thank Senior Research Analysts Jay Pilkerton, Pradeep Ramani and Jim Russo for their invaluable contributions to this blog.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Municipal Market Review

Tax-exempt municipal bonds capped off 2025 with a resilient fourth quarter, sustaining the momentum they gained in the prior three months as interest rates declined, tax-backed credit fundamentals remained solid, and demand ticked higher. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index gained 1.56% (including interest payments and price changes) for the quarter, helping munis offset a volatile first half of 2025 and lifting their full-year advance to 4.25%.

Munis notched most of their Q4 gain in October (+1.24%). That month, the U.S. Federal Reserve reduced its benchmark policy interest rate by a quarter percentage point (25 basis points), marking the central bank's second such rate cut in as many months, aimed at supporting a softening job market.

Improving technical factors were an additional tailwind. On the supply side, the pace of new bond issuance decelerated. Meanwhile, demand for munis strengthened, particularly among investors looking to lock in yields ahead of further anticipated interest-rate cuts. Moreover, passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July preserved the tax-exempt status of municipal bonds, removing a headwind that had created uncertainty in the muni market during the first half of 2025.

Munis then largely held steady in November (+0.23%) and December (+0.09%), supported by another quarter-point Federal Reserve rate reduction in December. Although new-issue volume remained elevated and contributed to a record year for issuance, it slowed toward year-end amid the typical seasonal lull in new deals. Supply was largely absorbed without market disruption amid firming investor demand, as healthy yields continued to attract buyers' attention.

Credit fundamentals for most tax-backed munis remained solid throughout the three months, with default rates very low and issuers' balance sheets generally healthy.

For the full quarter, long-duration municipals led the market, with 10-, 15-, 20- and 22-plus-year bonds posting the top gains (+1.74%, +2.84%, +2.20% and 1.63%, respectively), while one-, three-, five- and seven-year munis advanced 0.56%, 0.46%, 0.50% and 0.83%, respectively.

From a sector perspective, revenue bonds (+1.57%) performed roughly in line with general obligation bonds (+1.60%). Among GOs, local-government debt (+1.71%) modestly outpaced state-backed bonds (+1.44%). In the revenue category, there was little differentiation between the performance of various segments or ratings. Housing (+1.77%) and hospital bonds (+1.75%) fared best, while most other sectors followed closely behind – except for industrial development bonds, which returned only 0.65%.

By credit-quality rating, BBB-rated bonds gained 1.77%, slightly bettering returns in the 1.50% range notched by munis with higher ratings (AAA, AA, A).

Outlook and Positioning

As of December 31, yields on municipal bonds are still relatively high historically, offering tax-equivalent yields that compare favorably with U.S. Treasuries and corporate bonds, particularly for investors in high tax brackets.

As for market technicals, we believe new-issue supply may continue to remain high as issuers increase their borrowing due to higher costs and uncertainty around future federal funding. Against that backdrop, we believe investor demand will need to remain strong to absorb this higher level of expected issuance.

It is important for investors to remember that Fidelity's municipal bond portfolios are constructed with a careful and intentional emphasis on issue selection, especially with consideration to the liquidity of the security and the financial resiliency of its issuer. In the current environment, we continue to evaluate each fund holding and are monitoring those that may be more financially challenged than others.

Going forward, and as always, we will seek to identify opportunities to purchase municipal securities that offer both attractive valuations and a solid risk/reward profile. At quarter end, we continue to invest for strong risk-adjusted returns, while remaining cautious with yield-curve positioning, given the likelihood of ongoing interest-rate volatility.

We remain committed to the approach of building individual exposures in the portfolio that reflect risks with which we are comfortable, at entry prices we believe offer strong relative value.

Characteristics

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

30-day SEC Tax-Equivalent Yield shows what you would have to earn on a taxable investment to equal the fund's tax-free yield, if you are in the 37% effective federal income tax bracket and also subject to the 3.8% Medicare Contribution tax, but does not reflect the payment of the federal alternative minimum tax, if applicable. Medicare Contribution tax is a tax on non-municipal investment income that applies to individuals with incomes over $200,000 (or $250,000, filing jointly). For state-specific funds, TEY is based not only on the highest federal tax rate (40.8%) but also the highest state tax rate. For state-specific funds, TEYs assume investors are state residents and would not be able to take an itemized deduction on their federal returns for state taxes on investment income. For NY funds, TEYs do not reflect the NY state tax rate that applies to income in excess of $5 million. For MD funds, TEYs reflect the highest city/county tax rates in MD and treat them the same as state taxes. Consult a tax professional for further detail.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund;

determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

Subject to Alternative Minimum Tax is the percent of interest income subject to the alternative minimum tax (AMT) based on total net assets within a portfolio.
